danger_signals.visual_form: Undead Vikings, hostile creatures in level environments
danger_signals.meaning: Areas contain active threats requiring combat or avoidance

danger_signals.visual_form: Larger, distinct enemy sprites (Pete as rival antagonist)
danger_signals.meaning: Significant danger threshold; requires skill to progress

danger_signals.visual_form: Level design obstacles in overland and dungeon sections
Heart counter in lower-left corner decreasing
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat to player character state; death if depleted

safety_signals.visual_form: Popcorn and bubble gum scattered in levels
safety_signals.meaning: Resources available for progression and upgrades
Safe exit from overland section; checkpoint/respite from danger

safety_signals.visual_form: Spicy jalapeños in overland sections (Duckburg, India, Egypt)
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary invincibility and forward momentum available for player advantage

safety_signals.visual_form: Clues found in specific levels that unlock dungeon progression
safety_signals.meaning: Knowledge-based safety; proper preparation allows advancement

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Donald Duck character identity; nephews Huey, Dewey, Louie as supporting unit
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Biplane as team transport; represents domestic/familial faction

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Pete as antagonistic character; opposing treasure hunter
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Pete appears as boss encounter; visual distinction from other enemies
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Pete's hideout as faction stronghold; South Pole location as contested territory

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Count Dracula (Transylvania), Undead Vikings (ship), King Garuzia's guardians
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Faction-specific enemies tied to geographic regions and historical themes
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Dracula's castle (Transylvania), Viking ship, temple locations indicate faction control

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Donald traverses initial overland sections with fragmentary information
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can access levels in non-linear order; obstacles block dungeon entry until prerequisites met

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Donald obtains complete map; world becomes more strategically navigable
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Specific dungeon progression order becomes possible; world state unlocks new pathways

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Pete's faction controls South Pole location; dungeon threat escalates
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Forced pursuit mechanics; boss encounter with Pete becomes mandatory

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Final dungeon reveals King Garuzia's temple and statue
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Culmination level; ultimate boss/puzzle checkpoint before conclusion

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Statue's broken state reveals hidden golden necklace
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game conclusion; narrative reward state indicating successful mission completion

environmental_storytelling.example: Levels modeled after Mexico, Transylvania (Count Dracula), Viking ship, Egypt, India, South Pole; each region carries cultural/historical visual identity that communicates threat type and lore
Overland sections provide puzzle clues and context; dungeons contain Maharajah's palace, temples, and final treasure chamber; structure communicates exploration vs. confrontation phases
Gyro Gearloose provides bubble gum upgrades; Daisy's concern communicated through plot; Pete's rivalry established through narrative and encounter design
Incomplete map → complete map progression visually tracks player knowledge gain; diary acquisition in Viking ship signals shift in objective clarity
Undead Vikings in ship, Count Dracula in castle, Pete in hideout; boss locations reinforce faction control over geographic regions

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Standard platforming traversal, plunger-based combat, puzzle interaction
Invincibility activation, automatic forward momentum, enhanced knockback against enemies; temporary power state
Stuns regular enemies; ineffective against bosses; unlimited ammo signal of non-lethal approach
Functions as temporary wall-climbing platform; can attach to birds for traversal extension; mechanic evolution signals progression
Parallel pursuit of same objective; kidnapping nephews signals escalation; boss confrontation indicates faction conflict
Defend ship location; guarding diary; regional threat indicator for Viking-themed area
Operate biplane for safe level exit; held ransom triggers narrative crisis; non-combat allies indicating domestic faction

notes: QuackShot uses Indiana Jones-inspired adventure theming with geographic diversification (Transylvania, Egypt, India, Mexico, South Pole, Viking locale) to communicate world state through environmental design. The dual-phase level structure (overland exploration + dungeon confrontation) creates clear danger/safety transitions. Visual manifestation relies heavily on location-specific enemies (Dracula, Vikings, regional guardians) rather than faction color-coding. The incomplete-to-complete map arc is the primary world-state progression signal. Behavioral tells emphasize non-lethal combat initially (plungers) with power-up alternatives (jalapeños → invincibility), communicating game philosophy through ability evolution.
